title: Intracerebral Hemorrhage
authors:
J. Claude Hemphill, III,

Ediberto Amorim, Wade S. Smith

items.title: Role of CT Imaging in Acute ICH Diagnosis
items.content: CT imaging is the preferred method for diagnosing intracranial hemorrhage during acute stroke evaluation due to its high sensitivity, specificity, and wide availability. It helps determine the location of bleeding, which narrows the differential diagnosis.
keyPoints: CT imaging is highly sensitive and specific for detecting intracranial hemorrhage.
CT is preferred over MRI in acute stroke evaluations due to wider availability.
The location of hemorrhage identified on CT helps narrow the differential diagnosis.
items.reference: Intracerebral Hemorrhage · Intracerebral Hemorrhage | DIAGNOSIS | EMERGENCY MANAGEMENT

items.title: Blood Pressure Management in Acute ICH
items.content: Initial blood pressure should be maintained until CT confirms ICH. Recent trials suggest that lowering systolic blood pressure (SBP) to <140 mmHg is safe and may improve outcomes, though the optimal target remains debated.
keyPoints: Maintain initial BP until CT confirms ICH due to risk of hematoma expansion.
INTERACT2 trial showed a trend toward improved outcomes with SBP <140 mmHg compared to <180 mmHg (52% vs. 55.6%, p = .06).
Current guidelines suggest targeting an SBP of 130–150 mmHg in spontaneous ICH.
items.reference: Intracerebral Hemorrhage · Intracerebral Hemorrhage | DIAGNOSIS | EMERGENCY MANAGEMENT

items.title: Emergency Management of Elevated Intracranial Pressure (ICP)
items.content: Patients with signs of herniation or elevated ICP should be treated urgently. This includes tracheal intubation, sedation, osmotic diuretics, and head elevation while awaiting surgical consultation.
keyPoints: Use IV drugs like nicardipine for BP management to avoid cerebral vasodilation.
Maintain cerebral perfusion pressure (MAP minus ICP) of 60-≥70 mmHg in monitored patients.
Presumptive treatment for elevated ICP includes intubation, sedation, osmotic diuretics, and head elevation.
items.reference: Intracerebral Hemorrhage · Intracerebral Hemorrhage | DIAGNOSIS | EMERGENCY MANAGEMENT

items.title: Pathophysiology of Hypertensive Intracerebral Hemorrhage
items.content: Hypertensive intracerebral hemorrhage typically results from the spontaneous rupture of small penetrating arteries in deep brain regions. The most common sites include the basal ganglia, thalamus, cerebellum, and pons. These areas are prone to hypertension-induced vascular injury.
keyPoints: ICH accounts for ~10% of all strokes.
~35-45% of patients die within the first month.
Common sites: basal ganglia (especially putamen), thalamus, cerebellum, pons.
Consider other causes if hemorrhage occurs outside these areas or in nonhypertensive patients.

items.title: Clinical Manifestations and Progression of Hypertensive ICH
items.content: Hypertensive intracerebral hemorrhage presents with abrupt focal neurologic deficits. Seizures may occur in 6-15% within the first three days. Hemorrhages often worsen over 30–90 minutes, leading to increased intracranial pressure and potential herniation.
keyPoints: Abrupt onset of focal neurologic deficit.
Seizures: 6–15% within the first 3 days.
Worsening over 30–90 min with signs of increased ICP (headache, vomiting).
Putamen is the most common site; contralateral hemiparesis is a sentinel sign.

items.title: Clinical Features and Outcomes of Thalamic and Pontine Hemorrhages
items.content: Thalamic hemorrhages can cause contralateral hemiplegia or sensory deficits, with possible ocular disturbances. Pontine hemorrhages often lead to deep coma with quadriplegia and decerebrate rigidity, with a high mortality rate.
keyPoints: Thalamic hemorrhage: contralateral hemiplegia/hemiparesis, prominent sensory deficit, possible Déérine-Roussy syndrome.
Ocular disturbances in thalamic hemorrhages include skew deviation and ipsilateral Horner's syndrome.
Pontine hemorrhage: deep coma with quadriplegia, decerebrate rigidity, pinpoint pupils, high mortality.

items.title: Cerebellar Hemorrhage Presentation and Management
items.content: Cerebellar hemorrhages develop over several hours, presenting with occipital headache, vomiting, ataxia of gait, dizziness or vertigo, and possible ocular signs. As the condition progresses, brainstem compression may lead to stupor or coma, necessitating immediate surgical evacuation.
keyPoints: Symptoms include occipital headache, repeated vomiting, ataxia of gait, and paresis of conjugate lateral gaze.
Immediate surgical evacuation is lifesaving before severe brainstem compression occurs.
Full recovery is common if deep cerebellar nuclei are spared.

items.title: Lobar Hemorrhage Clinical Features
items.content: Lobar hemorrhages present with symptoms specific to the site of origin, such as hemianopsia for occipital hemorrhages and aphasia for left temporal hemorrhages. Large hemorrhages may lead to stupor or coma due to thalamic or midbrain compression.
keyPoints: Occipital hemorrhage presents with hemianopsia; left temporal with aphasia and confusion.
More than one-half of patients experience focal headaches, vomiting, or drowsiness.
Seizures may occur in lobar hemorrhages.

items.title: Causes and Diagnosis of Intracerebral Hemorrhage
items.content: Intracerebral hemorrhage can be caused by amyloid angiopathy, stimulant use, head injury, anticoagulant therapy, hematologic disorders, or brain tumors. Amyloid angiopathy is common in the elderly and associated with lobar hemorrhages.
keyPoints: Amyloid angiopathy causes single and recurrent lobar hemorrhages, often diagnosed by MRI and Congo red staining.
Stimulants like cocaine can lead to ICH through acute hypertension.
Anticoagulant-related ICHs may evolve over 24–48 hours; coagulopathy should be rapidly reversed.

items.title: Management of Hypertensive Encephalopathy vs. ICH
items.content: Hypertensive encephalopathy and hypertensive intracerebral hemorrhage (ICH) require different blood pressure management strategies due to their distinct pathophysiology.
keyPoints: In hypertensive encephalopathy, aggressive lowering of SBP is not recommended; target 130–150 mmHg acutely in ICH.
No alteration in mental status prior to ICH favors a diagnosis of hypertensive ICH over hypertensive encephalopathy.
MRI shows posterior brain edema in hypertensive encephalopathy, which can be reversible with proper management.

items.title: Investigation of Primary Intraventricular Hemorrhage
items.content: Primary intraventricular hemorrhage is rare and warrants investigation for underlying vascular anomalies, with angiography revealing sources in nearly half the cases.
keyPoints: Angiography identifies bleeding sources in about 50% of primary intraventricular hemorrhage cases.
Vasculitis can cause CNS hemorrhages; hypertension may exacerbate this by disrupting vessel walls.

items.title: Role of Imaging in Intracranial Hemorrhage Evaluation
items.content: CT imaging is essential for detecting acute hemorrhages, while MRI and angiography are used when the cause is uncertain.
keyPoints: CT reliably detects acute focal hemorrhages; MR angiography (MRA) and CT angiography (CTA) help identify causes of uncertain ICH.
The 'spot sign' on imaging indicates ongoing bleeding, associated with increased risk of hematoma expansion and mortality.

items.title: Management of Coagulopathy in ICH
items.content: In patients with intracerebral hemorrhage (ICH) who are on vitamin K antagonists, rapid correction of coagulopathy is crucial. Prothrombin complex concentrates (PCCs) should be used for quick reversal, alongside concurrent administration of vitamin K. For dabigatran, idarucizumab can quickly reverse its effects. Oral Xa inhibitors like apixaban and rivaroxaban can be reversed with andexanet alfa or partially with PCC if andexanet is unavailable.
keyPoints: Use PCCs for rapid reversal of coagulopathy in patients on VKAs, along with vitamin K.
Idarucizumab reverses dabigatran quickly.
Andexanet alfa reverses apixaban and rivaroxaban; use PCC if unavailable.

items.title: Blood Pressure Management in ICH
items.content: While blood pressure lowering is considered to prevent hematoma expansion, clinical trials have shown no significant difference in hematoma expansion between systolic blood pressure targets of 140–180 mmHg. However, more intensive blood pressure management may reduce hematoma expansion in deep hemorrhages involving the basal ganglia but does not improve functional outcomes.
keyPoints: No significant difference in hematoma expansion with SBP targets of 140–180 mmHg.
Intensive BP lowering reduces hematoma expansion in basal ganglia hemorrhages without improving function.

items.title: Surgical Management of ICH
items.content: Initial trials did not show clear benefits for early surgical evacuation of supratentorial hematomas. However, recent studies like the ENRICH trial indicate that minimally invasive hematoma removal within 24 hours can improve functional outcomes in selected patients with lobar hemorrhage (hematoma volume 30–80 mL; GCS 5–14). Surgical intervention remains an option for neurologically deteriorating patients.
keyPoints: Early surgical evacuation of supratentorial ICH showed no benefit initially.
ENRICH trial: Minimally invasive removal within 24 hours improves outcomes in selected lobar hemorrhage cases (hematoma volume 30–80 mL; GCS 5–14).
Surgery considered for patients with progressive neurologic deterioration.

items.title: Bundles of care improve outcomes in ICH management
items.content: Implementing a bundle of care interventions, such as coagulopathy reversal, blood pressure lowering, and neurosurgical referral, can significantly decrease mortality in intracerebral hemorrhage (ICH) patients. This approach is supported by findings from both a UK hospital quality assurance project and the INTERACT-3 trial.
keyPoints: A care bundle including coagulopathy reversal, blood pressure lowering, neurosurgical referral decreased patient mortality.
INTERACT-3 trial showed improved functional outcomes with additional interventions like vitamin K, glucose control, and temperature management.

items.title: Management of elevated ICP in ICH
items.content: Intracranial pressure (ICP) is often normal even with large intracerebral hemorrhages. However, if there's marked midline shift leading to obtundation or coma, osmotic agents and potential ventriculostomy are considered. Maintaining cerebral perfusion pressure between 60 to ≥70 mmHg is crucial, using interventions like CSF drainage and blood pressure management.
keyPoints: ICP often normal even with large ICHs; elevated ICP may require osmotic agents or ventriculostomy.
Maintain cerebral perfusion pressure (MAP minus ICP) of 60 to ≥70 mmHg.
Use CSF drainage and blood pressure management for high ICP.
Limit hyperventilation to acute resuscitation due to risk of ischemia.

items.title: Clinical Presentation and Risk Factors of AVMs
items.content: Arteriovenous malformations (AVMs) are congenital vascular anomalies that can lead to intracranial hemorrhage, headache, and seizures. They most commonly present between ages 10-30 but can occur as late as the fifties. Males are more frequently affected, and familial cases may be linked to hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia.
keyPoints: AVMs consist of a tangle of abnormal vessels that may raise cardiac output significantly.
Common presentations include headache, seizures, and intracranial hemorrhage.
Most common presentation age: 10-30 years; can occur up to the fifties.
Higher prevalence in men; familial cases linked to mutations affecting TGF signaling.

items.title: Hemorrhage Risk and Management of AVMs
items.content: The risk of hemorrhage from an unruptured AVM is approximately 2-4% per year, increasing to about 17% for the first year following a rupture. Surgical treatment is often indicated for accessible lesions presenting with hemorrhage, while asymptomatic AVM management remains controversial.
keyPoints: Unruptured AVMs have an annual hemorrhage rate of ~2-4%; previously ruptured AVMs may reach 17% in the first year post-rupture.
Surgical treatment is usually indicated for accessible lesions presenting with hemorrhage.
The ARUBA trial suggests medical management over intervention for asymptomatic AVMs due to higher complication rates.

items.title: Diagnostic and Predictive Features of AVMs
items.content: MRI is preferred over CT for diagnosing AVMs, though CT may detect calcification. Angiographic features like smaller lesion size, deep venous drainage, venous outflow stenosis, and intranidal aneurysms can predict higher bleeding risk.
keyPoints: MRI is superior to CT for AVM diagnosis; contrast-enhanced studies reveal abnormal vessels.
Smaller lesions paradoxically have a higher hemorrhage rate.
Predictive features of increased rupture risk include deep venous drainage, venous outflow stenosis, and intranidal aneurysms.

items.title: Understanding Cavernous Angiomas: Risks and Management
items.content: Cavernous angiomas, or cavernous malformations, are congenital capillary sinusoids often found in the deep hemispheric white matter and brainstem. They typically measure less than 1 cm and may be associated with venous anomalies. The annual bleeding risk for single cavernous malformations is between 0.7% to 1.5%, increasing with prior hemorrhage or multiple lesions. Surgical resection, which eliminates the bleeding risk and potentially reduces seizure risk, is generally reserved for surface malformations in patients with clinical episodes of bleeding or refractory seizures.
keyPoints: Cavernous angiomas are congenital capillary sinusoids typically <1 cm in diameter.
Annual bleeding risk: 0.7–1.5% per year, higher with prior hemorrhage or multiple malformations.
Surgical resection eliminates bleeding risk and may reduce seizure risk; reserved for surface lesions with clinical symptoms.

items.title: Cavernous Angiomas and Associated Venous Anomalies: Clinical Implications
items.content: Developmental venous anomalies, unlike arteriovenous malformations (AVMs), are functional venous channels with little clinical significance. They should be ignored if incidentally found on imaging. However, these anomalies can be associated with cavernous malformations, which do carry a bleeding risk. Surgical resection of developmental venous anomalies is risky and may lead to venous infarction or hemorrhage.
keyPoints: Developmental venous anomalies are functional venous channels often found incidentally on imaging.
Associated with cavernous malformations, which have a bleeding risk.
Surgical resection of venous anomalies can result in venous infarction and hemorrhage.

items.question: Which imaging modality is generally preferred for the acute evaluation of stroke due to its availability and logistical ease?
items.explanation: CT imaging is generally preferred for the acute evaluation of stroke because it is more widely available and may be logistically easier to perform than MRI.
items.reference: Intracerebral Hemorrhage · Intracerebral Hemorrhage | DIAGNOSIS | EMERGENCY MANAGEMENT

items.question: What was the primary outcome measure in the INTERACT2 trial regarding blood pressure management in spontaneous ICH?
items.explanation: The INTERACT2 trial focused on the effect of acute blood pressure lowering on functional outcomes in patients with spontaneous ICH.
items.reference: Intracerebral Hemorrhage · Intracerebral Hemorrhage | DIAGNOSIS | EMERGENCY MANAGEMENT

items.question: Which intravenous drugs are recommended for lowering blood pressure due to their minimal cerebral vasodilating action?
items.explanation: Nicardipine, clevidipine, labetalol, and esmolol are recommended for lowering blood pressure due to their minimal cerebral vasodilating action.
items.reference: Intracerebral Hemorrhage · Intracerebral Hemorrhage | DIAGNOSIS | EMERGENCY MANAGEMENT

items.question: What is the target systolic blood pressure (SBP) range endorsed by recent American Heart Association/American Stroke Association guidelines for managing spontaneous ICH?
items.explanation: The most recent guidelines endorse achieving and maintaining a target SBP of 130–150 mmHg in patients with spontaneous ICH.
items.reference: Intracerebral Hemorrhage · Intracerebral Hemorrhage | DIAGNOSIS | EMERGENCY MANAGEMENT

items.question: Which of the following is the most common site for hypertensive intracerebral hemorrhage?
items.explanation: The putamen is the most common site for hypertensive hemorrhage.

items.question: What is a typical ocular disturbance caused by thalamic hemorrhages?
options: Deviation of eyes downward and inward
items.explanation: Thalamic hemorrhages cause deviation of the eyes downward and inward.

items.question: Which symptom is commonly associated with pontine hemorrhage?
items.explanation: Pontine hemorrhages are associated with pinpoint pupils, decerebrate rigidity, and hyperpnea.

items.question: What percentage of patients with intracerebral hemorrhage die within the first month?
items.explanation: ~35-45% of patients die within the first month.

items.question: Which ocular sign is less frequently observed in cerebellar hemorrhages?
items.explanation: Less frequent ocular signs in cerebellar hemorrhages include blepharospasm, involuntary closure of one eye, ocular bobbing, and skew deviation.

items.question: What is a common initial symptom of lobar hemorrhage in the occipital region?
items.explanation: The major neurologic deficit with an occipital hemorrhage is hemianopsia.

items.question: What imaging finding can suggest amyloid angiopathy in the context of cerebral microbleeds?
items.explanation: 'Microbleeds' in the cortex, seen on brain MRI sequences sensitive for hemosiderin (susceptibility weighted imaging), suggest amyloid angiopathy.

items.question: Which genetic variations are associated with an increased risk of recurrent lobar hemorrhage in amyloid angiopathy?
items.explanation: The $ \varepsilon_2 $ and $ \varepsilon_4 $ allelic variations of the apolipoprotein E gene are associated with increased risk of recurrent lobar hemorrhage.

items.question: Which imaging modality reliably detects acute focal hemorrhages in the supratentorial space?
items.explanation: CT imaging is reliable for detecting acute focal hemorrhages in the supratentorial space.

items.question: What condition should prompt investigation for an underlying vascular anomaly when primary intraventricular hemorrhage occurs?
items.explanation: Primary intraventricular hemorrhage is rare and should prompt investigation for an underlying vascular anomaly.

items.question: Which condition may produce a posterior temporal or inferior parietal hematoma due to cortical vein hypertension?
items.explanation: Venous sinus thrombosis causes cortical vein hypertension, leading to a posterior temporal or inferior parietal hematoma.

items.question: What is the significance of the 'spot sign' in imaging studies?
options: It suggests ongoing bleeding and increased risk of hematoma expansion.
It shows complete recovery from intracerebral hemorrhage.
It confirms the presence of a coagulopathy.
items.explanation: The 'spot sign' is thought to represent ongoing bleeding and is associated with an increased risk of hematoma expansion.

items.question: Which agent is used for rapid reversal of anticoagulation effects of dabigatran in patients with intracerebral hemorrhage?
items.explanation: Idarucizumab is a monoclonal antibody used for the rapid reversal of dabigatran.

items.question: What is the recommended treatment for patients with intracerebral hemorrhage who are taking vitamin K antagonists?
options: Prothrombin complex concentrates (PCCs) with concurrent vitamin K administration
items.explanation: Rapid correction of coagulopathy in patients taking VKAs can be achieved by infusing PCCs, with vitamin K administered concurrently.

items.question: What is the recommended management for intracerebral hemorrhage associated with thrombocytopenia?
items.explanation: For ICH associated with thrombocytopenia (platelet count <50,000/μL), transfusion of fresh platelets is indicated.

items.question: What was the conclusion regarding tranexamic acid in a large randomized trial for intracerebral hemorrhage?
options: It reduced hematoma expansion but did not alter outcome.
It had no effect on hematoma expansion or clinical outcomes.
It increased the risk of hematoma expansion.
items.explanation: The administration of tranexamic acid was not found to alter outcome in a large randomized trial for intracerebral hemorrhage.

items.question: Which intervention is NOT recommended for the treatment of intracerebral hemorrhage?
items.explanation: Glucocorticoids are not recommended for the treatment of intracerebral hemorrhage.

items.question: In managing a patient with high ICP due to intracerebral hemorrhage, which intervention should be limited and eventually eliminated once osmotic therapy or surgical treatments have been instituted?
items.explanation: Induced hyperventilation should be limited to acute resuscitation and eliminated once osmotic therapy or surgical treatments have been instituted.

items.question: What is the target cerebral perfusion pressure (CPP) range for managing patients with intracerebral hemorrhage?
items.explanation: The target CPP range is 60 to ≥70 mmHg, calculated as MAP minus ICP.

items.question: Which imaging modality is considered the gold standard for evaluating the precise anatomy of an arteriovenous malformation (AVM)?
items.explanation: Once identified, conventional x-ray angiography is the gold standard for evaluating the precise anatomy of the AVM.

items.question: What is a common symptom that may occur in approximately 30% of patients with arteriovenous malformations (AVMs)?
options: Focal seizures, with or without generalization
items.explanation: Focal seizures, with or without generalization, occur in ~30% of cases.

items.question: What is the approximate annual hemorrhage rate for unruptured arteriovenous malformations (AVMs)?
items.explanation: Unruptured AVMs have a hemorrhage rate of ~2–4% per year.

items.question: What is the most common location for large arteriovenous malformations (AVMs) in the brain?
items.explanation: The largest AVMs are most frequently located in the posterior half of the hemispheres.

items.question: Which genes have been mapped to familial cavernous angiomas?
items.explanation: Familial cavernous angiomas have been mapped to the genes KRIT1, CCM2, and PDCD10.

items.question: What is the typical size of cavernous angiomas?
items.explanation: Cavernous angiomas are typically <1 cm in diameter.

items.question: What is the annual bleeding risk for single cavernous malformations?
items.explanation: The bleeding risk for single cavernous malformations is 0.7–1.5% per year.

items.statement: The INTERACT2 trial demonstrated a statistically significant difference in mortality between the two blood pressure target groups.
items.explanation: Both groups had similar mortality rates, although there was a shift to improved outcomes in the lower blood pressure arm.
items.reference: Intracerebral Hemorrhage · Intracerebral Hemorrhage | DIAGNOSIS | EMERGENCY MANAGEMENT

items.statement: Patients with ICP monitors should maintain cerebral perfusion pressure of 60 to ≥70 mmHg.
items.explanation: Maintaining a cerebral perfusion pressure of 60 to ≥70 mmHg is reasonable depending on the patient's cerebral autoregulation status.
items.reference: Intracerebral Hemorrhage · Intracerebral Hemorrhage | DIAGNOSIS | EMERGENCY MANAGEMENT

items.statement: Seizures are common at presentation in intracerebral hemorrhage.
items.explanation: Seizures are uncommon on presentation but may occur within the first 3 days.

items.statement: Hypertension is a common cause of intracerebral hemorrhage.
items.explanation: Hypertension causes most hypertensive intracerebral hemorrhages.

items.statement: Cerebellar hemorrhages can lead to immediate coma due to brainstem compression.
items.explanation: As the hours pass, patients with cerebellar hemorrhage often become stuporous and then comatose from brainstem compression.

items.statement: Oral anticoagulants are typically recommended for managing cerebral amyloid angiopathy.
items.explanation: Noninflammatory CAA has no specific treatment, and oral anticoagulants are typically avoided.

items.statement: Lumbar puncture is generally necessary for patients with intracerebral hemorrhage to assess cerebrospinal fluid.
items.explanation: A lumbar puncture is usually unnecessary and should be avoided due to the risk of cerebral herniation.

items.statement: MRI is generally required for the primary diagnosis of intracranial hemorrhage.
items.explanation: MRI is not necessary for primary diagnosis; CT imaging is typically sufficient.

items.statement: Clinical trials have demonstrated that the administration of recombinant factor VIIa improves clinical outcomes in patients with intracerebral hemorrhage.
items.explanation: A phase 3 trial showed reduced hematoma expansion but no improvement in clinical outcomes, so its use is not recommended.

items.statement: The ENRICH trial found that early surgical removal of lobar hematomas within 24 hours improved functional outcomes compared to medical management alone.
items.explanation: The ENRICH trial demonstrated improvement in functional outcome after surgical hematoma evacuation.

items.statement: Oral anticoagulant medications are generally recommended for patients with high-risk features for CAA.
items.explanation: Oral anticoagulants should be avoided in patients with high-risk features for cerebral amyloid angiopathy (CAA).

items.statement: Hypertension is the leading cause of primary intracerebral hemorrhage.
items.explanation: Prevention strategies focus on reducing chronic hypertension to prevent ICH.

items.statement: Stereotactic radiosurgery can produce a slow sclerosis of an arteriovenous malformation (AVM) over 2–3 years.
items.explanation: Stereotactic radiosurgery is an alternative to conventional surgery that can slowly cause AVM sclerosis over time.

items.statement: The ARUBA trial concluded that intervention for unruptured brain arteriovenous malformations was beneficial compared to medical management.
items.explanation: The ARUBA trial showed harm in the intervention group, with a higher rate of death or symptomatic stroke compared to medical management.

items.statement: Surgical resection of developmental venous anomalies may result in venous infarction and hemorrhage.
items.explanation: Surgical resection of these anomalies can lead to venous infarction and hemorrhage.

items.statement: Intracranial hemorrhage from cavernous malformations is increased with antiplatelet and anticoagulant medications.
items.explanation: Retrospective data show that intracranial hemorrhage from cavernous malformations is not increased with these medications.

items.assertion: Rapid reversal of coagulopathy is crucial in the management of spontaneous ICH.
items.reason: It ideally should occur within 1 hour of presentation to prevent further complications.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why rapid reversal of coagulopathy is important.
items.reference: Intracerebral Hemorrhage · Intracerebral Hemorrhage | DIAGNOSIS | EMERGENCY MANAGEMENT

items.assertion: The ATACH2 trial showed that aggressive blood pressure lowering reduces hematoma growth.
items.reason: Aggressive blood pressure lowering was associated with increased renal adverse events in the trial.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the ATACH2 trial did not demonstrate a reduction in hematoma growth, but it did show an increase in renal adverse events.
items.reference: Intracerebral Hemorrhage · Intracerebral Hemorrhage | DIAGNOSIS | EMERGENCY MANAGEMENT

items.assertion: Most hypertensive ICHs develop over 30–90 minutes.
items.reason: Hypertensive ICH usually results from spontaneous rupture of a small penetrating artery deep in the brain.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain the time frame for development.

items.assertion: Thalamic hemorrhages can cause a homonymous visual field defect.
items.reason: Thalamic hemorrhages extend inferiorly into the upper midbrain causing ocular disturbances.
items.explanation: Both are true, and the reason explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Cocaine use is associated with intracranial hemorrhage in young patients.
items.reason: Cocaine enhances sympathetic activity causing acute hypertension, which may lead to hemorrhage.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the association.

items.assertion: Head injury is a common cause of intracranial hemorrhage.
items.reason: Trauma must be considered in patients with acute neurologic deficits, especially after a fall.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why trauma should be considered.

items.assertion: Hypertensive encephalopathy should be distinguished from hypertensive ICH.
items.reason: Aggressive lowering of SBP is often considered in hypertensive ICH, but less aggressive measures are used in hypertensive encephalopathy.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why distinguishing between these conditions is important.

items.assertion: Venous sinus thrombosis should be treated with anticoagulation despite the presence of hemorrhage.
items.reason: Anticoagulation reduces venous hypertension and limits further bleeding.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why anticoagulation is indicated.

items.assertion: Minimally invasive surgical techniques for intracerebral hemorrhage are promising.
items.reason: Recent clinical trials have shown improved outcomes with these techniques compared to traditional methods.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason is not the correct explanation as recent focus holds promise without specific outcome comparisons mentioned.

items.assertion: Patients with cerebellar hemorrhages and obstructive hydrocephalus should be managed surgically.
items.reason: Surgical evacuation is generally required for hydrocephalus due to cerebellar hematoma.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why surgical management is necessary.

items.assertion: Smaller arteriovenous malformations (AVMs) have a paradoxically higher hemorrhage rate.
items.reason: The presence of deep venous drainage, venous outflow stenosis, and intranidal aneurysms may increase rupture risk.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true but unrelated; smaller AVMs have a higher hemorrhage rate regardless of these angiographic features.

items.assertion: The ARUBA trial supports routine intervention for patients with unruptured brain arteriovenous malformations.
items.reason: The medical arm in the ARUBA trial achieved a lower rate of death or symptomatic stroke compared to the intervention group.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the ARUBA trial argues against routine intervention, as it showed higher adverse outcomes in the intervention group.
